items.topic_thesis: Internal collapse and competitive collapse are distinct failure modes.
items.angle: Open on Writer survey: AI adoption is tearing companies apart in plain language, then show how it makes the deeper diagnosis legible.
items.hook: The most immediate AI threat in many companies may not be losing the market. It may be breaking the organization first.
items.timely_subject: Writer survey: AI adoption is tearing companies apart

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Internal collapse and competitive collapse are distinct failure modes.
publish_payload.angle: Open on Writer survey: AI adoption is tearing companies apart in plain language, then show how it makes the deeper diagnosis legible.
publish_payload.hook: Writer’s latest survey found a majority of executives saying AI adoption is pulling their company apart. Read that literally. Not “change is hard.” Power struggles, IT-business conflict, and employee resistance severe enough to feel like internal rupture.
publish_payload.body_text:
Writer’s latest survey found a majority of executives saying AI adoption is pulling their company apart. Read that literally. Not “change is hard.” Power struggles, IT-business conflict, and employee resistance severe enough to feel like internal rupture.

That makes one important distinction legible: internal collapse and competitive collapse are different failure modes.

Competitive collapse is when the market moves faster than you do. Internal collapse is when you try to keep up by burning the trust that lets the company learn. From the outside, both can look like urgency. Only one leaves the organization intact.

AI makes this easier to see because it changes status before it changes process. People are not only reacting to tools. They are reacting to what the tools do to authority, identity, and decision rights.

A serious operator should track both scoreboards. Don’t ask only whether adoption is fast enough. Ask whether the company can absorb the shock without turning truth into politics. If not, speed is not strategy. It is liquidation.
publish_payload.footer_text: The Dionysus Program is free. It covers the machinery behind posts like this — Run Time, Ritual Time, the anti-scapegoat, and how to make criticism cheaper than silence. dionysusprogram.com

items.seed_topic_thesis: To found trust without building a cult, leaders must transgress, recognize, release, and submit.

x_publish_payload.text:
AI adoption is exposing a harder failure than falling behind: companies breaking themselves to move faster.

Markets can outrun you. But if speed burns trust, truth becomes politics and the org stops learning. That isn’t transformation. It’s liquidation.

items.topic_thesis: Living law has to submit itself to the same anti-scapegoat logic it imposes on everyone else.
items.angle: Use the note as the entry point, then get to the pattern it reveals immediately.
items.hook: A note landed in my inbox that names a pattern I keep seeing. living law has to submit itself to the same anti-scapegoat logic it imposes on everyone else.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Living law has to submit itself to the same anti-scapegoat logic it imposes on everyone else.
publish_payload.angle: Use the note as the entry point, then get to the pattern it reveals immediately.
publish_payload.hook: A conduct code that cannot be put on trial will become a sacrifice machine.
publish_payload.body_text:
A conduct code that cannot be put on trial will become a sacrifice machine.

Not Carl Jung caught the tell: “turning your priests into tyrants.” That’s what happens when the people administering anti-scapegoat rules are exempt from the same discipline they impose on everyone else.

The pattern is old. A law is written to stop blame from collapsing onto a person. Then, over time, the law itself becomes the shield behind which stewards punish downward, freeze categories, and call that protection. The ritual stays. The victim just gets cleaner.

So living law has to submit itself to the same anti-scapegoat logic it imposes on the group. Put the code on trial. Put the tribunal on trial. Ask whether atimia is being weaponized, whether restoration is real, whether the container is still protecting people instead of power.

If your law can judge everyone but cannot itself be judged, it is already dead.
publish_payload.footer_text: The Dionysus Program is free. It covers the machinery behind posts like this — Run Time, Ritual Time, the anti-scapegoat, and how to make criticism cheaper than silence. dionysusprogram.com

x_publish_payload.text:
When a conduct code cannot be put on trial, it stops correcting abuse and starts manufacturing it.

The enforcers become untouchable, categories harden, and punishment hides inside procedure. The law survives. Living judgment doesn’t.

items.topic_thesis: The faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.
items.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: The faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.
items.hook: The mistake leaders keep making is thinking the faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: The faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.
publish_payload.angle: Name the concept, explain it plainly, and show why it matters now. Keep the framing specific to this thesis: The faster knowledge changes, the more an organization needs a stable myth layer.
publish_payload.hook: The faster your knowledge expires, the more dangerous it is to build identity on expertise.
publish_payload.body_text:
The faster your knowledge expires, the more dangerous it is to build identity on expertise.

The distinction is simple: the knowledge layer explains the world; the myth layer explains you. Knowledge makes bets reality can falsify. Myth carries stories about how to be, not what is true. The Cincinnatus story still works every time someone serves and returns to the plow.

When knowledge was durable, organizations could mostly ignore this. People anchored to what they knew because what they knew lasted. Now tools, roles, and assumptions can go stale in months. If identity sits on the knowledge layer, every update feels like a status loss.

That is why myth is not narrative decoration. It is infrastructure. In a high-change environment, you need a stable myth layer under the knowledge layer, or the company will start defending obsolete knowledge just to preserve itself.
publish_payload.footer_text: I released The Dionysus Program for free because ideas improve under public stress, not behind paywalls. Read it, critique it, kill what does not work. dionysusprogram.com
publish_payload.final_text:
The faster your knowledge expires, the more dangerous it is to build identity on expertise.

The distinction is simple: the knowledge layer explains the world; the myth layer explains you. Knowledge makes bets reality can falsify. Myth carries stories about how to be, not what is true. The Cincinnatus story still works every time someone serves and returns to the plow.

When knowledge was durable, organizations could mostly ignore this. People anchored to what they knew because what they knew lasted. Now tools, roles, and assumptions can go stale in months. If identity sits on the knowledge layer, every update feels like a status loss.

That is why myth is not narrative decoration. It is infrastructure. In a high-change environment, you need a stable myth layer under the knowledge layer, or the company will start defending obsolete knowledge just to preserve itself.

x_publish_payload.text:
Knowledge tells you what is true. Myth tells you who you are.

In a slow world, those could blur. In a fast one, they can't. Tools go stale in months; identity needs a deeper anchor.

Without it, people defend yesterday's knowledge as themselves.

items.topic_thesis: Apollo and Dionysus are complements, not substitutes: one optimizes the known system, the other governs death and rebirth.
items.angle: Start with the social cost of missing the pattern, then classify it clearly.
items.hook: You can tell a team is drifting into theater when apollo and dionysus are complements, not substitutes: one optimizes the known system, the other governs death and rebirth.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: Apollo and Dionysus are complements, not substitutes: one optimizes the known system, the other governs death and rebirth.
publish_payload.angle: Start with the social cost of missing the pattern, then classify it clearly.
publish_payload.hook: The social cost of missing this pattern is brutal: the dashboard gets sharper while the room gets colder.
publish_payload.body_text:
The social cost of missing this pattern is brutal: the dashboard gets sharper while the room gets colder.

That’s the Apollo-only organization. It knows how to optimize the known system, but it has no safe way to let an obsolete one die.

You see it when uncertainty gets hidden until it shows up as waste, when postmortems turn polite, when people over-insure every plan to protect themselves. On a night paving job, the trucks stack up and idle in the dark. By then it’s too late. The waste was decided hours earlier, when over-ordering was the only socially admissible move.

What drives it is a category error. Apollo and Dionysus are not substitutes. Apollo governs Run Time: measure, predict, control. Dionysus governs death and rebirth: the public killing of bad explanations without sacrificing the people who held them.

Ignore that, and you become efficient at defending yesterday while tomorrow burns in the dark.
publish_payload.footer_text: Most of what I post here is extracted from The Dionysus Program — a free field manual for leaders whose knowledge is melting faster than their culture can re-bind it. dionysusprogram.com
publish_payload.final_text:

Operators often think they have a forecasting problem. What they usually have is a permission problem.

If the only socially admissible move is to over-insure the plan, waste is decided upstream. The trucks idling in the dark are just the receipt.

items.topic_thesis: The Weekly Crossing is a practical recurring ritual for metabolizing drift before it hardens.
items.angle: Start with the pressure the team feels, then show the move that lowers the social cost of truth.
items.hook: If your team keeps repeating the same failure, stop adding process. the weekly crossing is a practical recurring ritual for metabolizing drift before it hardens.

publish_payload.topic_thesis: The Weekly Crossing is a practical recurring ritual for metabolizing drift before it hardens.
publish_payload.angle: Start with the pressure the team feels, then show the move that lowers the social cost of truth.
publish_payload.hook: You can feel drift before the dashboard can prove it.
publish_payload.body_text:
You can feel drift before the dashboard can prove it.

On a night paving job, trucks back up and idle in the dark because the real decision was made hours earlier: under uncertainty, the only socially safe move was to over-order. That pattern shows up everywhere. The team feels it first. Nobody wants to be the one who names the plan as wrong while it is still reversible.

The common mistake is to wait for visible failure, or to ask for “more candor” inside a normal status meeting. By then drift has already hardened. What was once awkward to question is now normal, then moral.

Run a Weekly Crossing instead. Same short block every week. Declare: no person is on trial. Put one artifact on the table—a plan, metric, policy, forecast. Critics go first. Owner speaks last. Close with one commitment and one piece of evidence that would overturn it.

Do not use this on an exhausted or low-trust team. Cool the system first. Otherwise you get theater.

If truth only becomes speakable after the miss, you are already paying full price.
publish_payload.footer_text: Nothing in this post requires you to read The Dionysus Program. But if you want the operating system underneath it — the full loop, the diagnostics, the recipes — it is free. dionysusprogram.com
